item.title: 这周你的胜利是什么？ | What was your win this week?
item.description:
开发团队的Michael Tharrington

发表于 6 月 30 日

嘿伙计们👋

希望大家周末愉快。

回顾过去的一周，您完成了哪些值得自豪的事情？

所有胜利都很重要——无论大小🎉

开始一个新项目

  • 修复一个棘手的错误
  • 创建独立游戏👾

    • 一旦被暂停，开发团队将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到其暂停被解除。

    一旦解除暂停，开发团队将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

    一旦取消发布，开发团队的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己才能访问。

    如果开发团队没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从仪表板重新发布帖子。

    笔记：

    感谢您保证 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以执行以下操作来标记开发团队：

    使开发团队的所有帖子不那么明显

    举报其他不当行为

    坚持不懈的开发团队将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



    原文:

    Michael Tharrington for The DEV Team

    Posted on Jun 30

     

    Hey folks 👋

    Hope that y'all all enjoy your weekends.

    Looking back on this past week, what was something you were proud of accomplishing?

    All wins count — big or small 🎉

    Examples of 'wins' include:

    • Starting a new project
    • Fixing a tricky bug
    • Creating an indie game 👾

    Once unpublished, all posts by devteam will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

    If devteam is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

    Thanks for keeping DEV Community safe. Here is what you can do to flag devteam:

    Make all posts by devteam less visible

    devteam consistently posts content that violates DEV Community's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

    Report other inappropriate conduct

    Unflagging devteam will restore default visibility to their posts.

item.title: 2023 年开发者使用的前 5 名前后端技术 | Top 5 Frontend and Backend Technologies Used by Developers in 2023
item.description:
德鲁夫·乔希

发表于 6 月 30 日

前端开发人员和后端开发人员必须随时了解技术和框架的趋势，以及使用哪种技术和框架来实现高效、更快的开发。 保持最新状态也使他们在市场上具有竞争力。 但是，有时很难保持更新。

所以，不用担心； 我们收集了前 5 个前端和后端开发框架，并描述了为什么您应该为您的项目联系前端或后端开发服务。

让我们深入研究而不浪费时间。

让我们看看您可以纳入开发流程并增强应用程序开发的 5 种最重要的后端和前端技术。

前端开发人员负责网站或应用程序的外观和印象，以及用户界面的功能。 这些技术广泛应用于电子商务、社交媒体、金融等各个行业。

让我们快速了解一下这些技术及其优缺点。

它允许开发人员创建可重用的组件，使开发过程更加高效。 它还具有很强的适应性，使其成为大型复杂项目的不错选择。 使用 React 的顶级公司

  • 彭博社
  • 优食
  • 不和谐
  • 兴趣
  • 销售队伍

很容易找到好的开发商
  • 创建跨平台应用程序
  • 大社区
  • 向后兼容性
  • 可重复使用的组件
  • 虚拟DOM

    • 基于第三方库
  • 没有约定

    • 角

    众所周知，Angular 是一个用于前端 Web 开发的 JavaScript 框架。 它是一个完整的前端开发解决方案，提供用于制作动态和交互式用户界面的工具和功能。 使用 Angular 的顶级公司

    • 微软办公软件
    • 德意志银行
    • 混合器
    • 桑坦德银行
    • 邮箱
    • 福布斯
    • 向上工作
    • 贝宝

    角度的优点

    • 增强的设计架构
    • 模块
    • 定制指令
    • 服务和依赖注入

    复杂且冗长
  • 有限的搜索引擎优化选项
  • 有点困难的学习曲线
  • 我们都知道它是一个用于创建用户界面的 JavaScript 框架。 它与 React 类似，都允许创建可重用组件，但它被认为具有更温和的学习曲线。 使用 Vue.Js 的顶级公司

    • 小米
    • 土坯
    • 杂七杂八的傻瓜
    • 特里瓦戈
    • 语法
    • 简单易用的虚拟 DOM 直观的 API

      不错的文档

    语言限制
  • 没有简化的方法
  • 用户基数较少

    • 它基于 React 和 Webpack 构建，允许开发人员创建 SEO 友好且高性能的网页。 使用 Next.js 的顶级公司

    • 沃尔玛
    • 苹果
    • 耐克
    • 抖音
    • 优步
    • 来福车
    • 星巴克

    卓越的性能，出色的加载时间
  • 伟大的搜索引擎优化
  • 最佳用户体验
  • 对开发者的良好支持

    • 对于开发者来说很难学习

    盖茨比

    它使用 React 和 GraphQL 在构建过程中生成静态页面，这可以提高性能和更好的 SEO。 它有利于创建网站和博客，并且在想要实现快速、流畅的用户体验的前端开发人员中很受欢迎。

    使用 Gatsby 的顶级公司

    • 快照
    • 火种
    • 堆
    • 革命
    • 这儿付款
    • 氧化实验室
    • 马克IT
    • 确认

    盖茨比的优点

    • 页面加载速度快
    • 简单易学
    • 搜索引擎优化效率
    • 安全
    • 面向未来
    • 更多潜在客户和客户
    • 良好的用户体验
    • 结构化数据
    • 活跃的社区
    • 盖茨比的缺点
      • 需要大量更新内容
      • 没有即插即用功能
      • 没有 WordPress 风格的插件

      后端开发涉及创建网站或应用程序的服务器端逻辑和数据管理。 作为后端开发人员，您将负责制作与前端交互的 API 和数据库。 这些技术广泛应用于电子商务、社交媒体、金融等各个行业。

      让我们快速了解一下这些技术及其优缺点。 在继续之前，您可以联系后端开发服务提供商，以无缝地完成您的项目，而无需担心。

      它使其成为已经熟悉 JavaScript 的后端开发人员的理想选择。 如果您有一个开源项目，您可以聘请一名 Node.js 开发人员。

      使用 Node.js 的顶级公司

      • 领英
      • 优步
      • 特雷洛
      • 贝宝
      • 美国宇航局
      • 易趣
      • 中等的

      令人印象深刻吧？ 联系后端开发服务提供商，这样您就可以像这些公司一样取得成功。

      强大的技术堆栈

    • 可针对微服务进行扩展
    • 丰富的生态系统
    • 强大的企业支持
    • 大型社区

    一些繁重任务的瓶颈
  • 回调地狱问题
  • 工具不成熟
  • 不良的依赖管理

    • 它拥有一个庞大而活跃的社区，这意味着有大量的库和框架可供Python开发人员使用。 它也是机器学习和数据分析的流行选择。

    使用 Python 的顶级公司

    • 英特尔
    • 国际商业机器公司
    • 美国宇航局
    • 皮克斯
    • 摩根大通
    • 灵活且可扩展
    • 大型社区
    • 丰富的图书馆
    • 高度可扩展
    • 可嵌入

    设计上的一些问题
  • 一些安全问题
  • 内存消耗高
  • 复杂的多线程
  • 比编译语言慢

    • 红宝石 on Rails

    它是一个用 Ruby 编写的 Web 应用程序框架。 它遵循约定的配置理念，可以使后端开发过程更快、更高效。 使用 Ruby on Rails 的顶级公司

    • 紧缩基地
    • 推特
    • 埃特西
    • 购物
    • 爱彼迎
    • 询问.fm
    • 极品

    时间效率
  • 庞大而活跃的社区
  • 大量有用的工具和库
  • 严格遵守标准

    • 灵活性较差
  • 需要不断发展
  • 启动时间性能较差
  • 错误将会付出高昂的代价

    • 它简约且灵活，使开发人员可以轻松创建和维护 Web 应用程序。 使用 Express.Js 的顶级公司

    • 推特
    • 埃森哲
    • 堆
    • 凯文
    • 客户端平台
    • 财捷
    • 信任飞行员
    • 普拉蒂利皮

    快速应用程序开发
  • 开源和大社区
  • 轻松集成第三方服务
  • 简单易学

    • 事件驱动性质（回调）
  • 代码组织
  • 有些错误消息没有帮助
  • 安全性不太好

    • 姜戈

    众所周知，Django 是一个高级 Python Web 框架，鼓励快速开发和完美、务实的设计。 它构建在 Python 之上，通常用于大型组织中的Web 开发。 它还提供内置管理界面，使开发人员可以快速创建用于管理应用程序数据的后端。

    使用 Django 的顶级公司

    • 国家地理
    • 莫兹拉
    • 兴趣
    • 讨论
    • 位桶
    • 事件布里特

    用Python实现
  • 更好的内容管理和更好的 CDN
  • 快速处理
  • 快速发展
  • 可扩展
  • 安全

    • 姜戈的缺点

    • 不适合较小的项目
    • 它可能会导致网站速度缓慢
    • 缺乏约定

    最后的话

    行业在不断变化，后端和前端开发人员必须随时了解情况并适应变化，以提供高质量的产品和服务。 您可以通过了解最新的后端和前端 Web 开发趋势和工具来增强开发人员的发展。

    假设您对网络或移动应用程序开发有独特的想法，并寻求一个真正值得信赖的合作伙伴。

    在这种情况下，您可以根据需要联系前端开发服务和后端开发服务。

    记得点赞、关注、评论、分享哦！ 谢谢阅读！

    举报其他不当行为

    取消标记 dhruvjoshi9 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



    原文:

    Dhruv Joshi

    Posted on Jun 30

     

    Frontend developers and backend developers must stay updated with the trend of technologies and frameworks and which one to use for efficient and faster development. Staying up to date also makes them competitive in the market. But, It's hard to stay updated sometimes.

    So, don't worry; we have collected the top 5 frameworks for frontend and backend development and described why you should contact frontend or backend development services for your project.

    Let's dive in without wasting time.

    Let's look at the top 5 backend and frontend technologies you can incorporate into your development process and enhance your app development.

    Top 5 Frontend Technologies Developers Are Using in 2023

    Frontend development is about creating an engaging and interactive user experience for the end-users. Frontend developers are responsible for the appearance and impression of the website or application, as well as the functionality of the user interface. In 2023, developers' most popular frontend technologies are React, Angular, and Vue.js. These technologies are widely used in various industries, such as e-commerce, social media, and finance.

    Let's quickly examine these technologies and their pros and cons.

    React (The Most Commonly Used Framework)

    React is a JavaScript library for creating UIs. It allows developers to create reusable components, making the development process more efficient. It is also highly adaptable, making it a good choice for large and complex projects. React has a large and active community, which means plenty of resources are available for developers to learn and use.

    Top Companies Using React

    • Bloomberg
    • Facebook
    • Uber Eats
    • Discord
    • Instagram
    • Skype
    • Pinterest
    • Salesforce

    Pros of React

    • Very easy to find good developers
    • Creates cross-platform apps
    • Big Community
    • Backward Compatibility
    • Reusable components
    • Virtual DOM

    Cons of React

    • Based on 3rd party libraries
    • No convention

    Angular

    As we all know, Angular is a JavaScript framework for front web development. It is a complete frontend development solution, providing tools and features for making dynamic and interactive user interfaces. Angular is beneficial for creating complex and large-scale projects and is a popular choice for enterprise-level applications.

    Top Companies Using Angular

    • Microsoft Office
    • Deutsche Bank
    • Mixer
    • Santander
    • Gmail
    • Forbes
    • Upwork
    • PayPal

    Pros of Angular

    • MVC Architecture Implementation
    • Enhanced Design Architecture
    • Modules
    • Custom Directives
    • Services and Dependency Injection

    Cons of Angular

    • Complex and Verbose
    • Limited SEO options
    • The little hard learning curve
    • Lack of details in CLI documentation

    Vue.js

    It's a JavaScript framework that we all know for creating user interfaces. It is similar to React in that it allows for the creation of reusable components, but it is considered to have a gentler learning curve. Vue.js is highly adaptable and easily integrated with other technologies, making it a popular choice among frontend development services.

    Top Companies Using Vue.Js

    • Facebook
    • Netflix
    • Xiaomi
    • Adobe
    • The Motley Fool
    • Trivago
    • Grammarly
    • Gitlab

    Pros of Vue.Js

    Simple and Easy Virtual DOM Intuitive APIs

    Nice Documentation

    Cons of Vue.Js

    • Language Constraint
    • No Streamlined Approach
    • Fewer user bases

    Next.Js

    Next.js is a JavaScript framework for creating server-rendered React apps. It is built on React and Webpack, allowing developers to create SEO-friendly and performant web pages. Next.js also provides features like automatic code splitting and easy integration with other technologies, making it popular among frontend development services.

    Top Companies Using Next.js

    • Walmart
    • Apple
    • Nike
    • Netflix
    • TikTok
    • Uber
    • Lyft
    • Starbucks

    Pros of Next.Js

    • Excellent performance, great load times
    • Great SEO
    • Best user experience
    • Good support for developers

    Cons of Next.Js

    • Hard to learn for developers

    Gatsby

    Gatsby is a JavaScript framework for building static and progressive web applications. It uses React and GraphQL to generate static pages during the build process, which can lead to improved performance and better SEO. Gatsby also provides a rich set of plugins and APIs for handling various frontend tasks, such as image optimization, offline support, and analytics. It is beneficial for creating websites and blogs and is popular among frontend developers who want to make fast and smooth user experiences.

    Top Companies Using Gatsby

    • Snapchat
    • Tinder
    • Stack
    • Revolut
    • Payhere
    • Oxylabs
    • MAK IT
    • Affirm

    Pros of Gatsby

    • Fast page loading speed
    • Easy to learn
    • SEO efficiency
    • Security
    • Future-proof
    • More leads and customers
    • Good user experience
    • Structured Data
    • Active community
    • East for CI/CD

    Cons of Gatsby

    • Need to update content a lot
    • No plug-and-play functionality
    • No WordPress-style plugins

    Top 5 Backend Technologies Developers Are Using in 2023

    Backend development involves creating a website or application's server-side logic and data management. As a backend developer, you will be accountable for making the APIs and databases that the frontend will interact with. In 2023, developers' most popular backend technologies are Node.js, Python, and Ruby on Rails. These technologies are widely used in various industries, such as e-commerce, social media, and finance.

    Let's quickly examine these technologies and their pros and cons. Before proceeding, you can reach out to a backend development services provider to get your project done seamlessly without worrying.

    Node.Js

    Node.js is a JavaScript-based runtime environment that lets developers run JavaScript on the server side. It makes it an ideal choice for backend developers already familiar with JavaScript. Node.js allows for easy scalability and is commonly used in real-time applications like chat and gaming. If you have a lit project, you can hire a Node.js developer.

    Top Companies Using Node.js

    • LinkedIn
    • Netflix
    • Uber
    • Trello
    • PayPal
    • NASA
    • eBay
    • Medium

    Impressive right? Reach out to a backend development services provider so you can succeed as these companies have.

    Pros of Node.Js

    • Robust tech stack
    • Fast process for web tasks
    • Scalable for microservices
    • Rich ecosystem
    • Strong corporate support
    • JSON support
    • Large community

    Cons of Node.Js

    • Bottleneck with some heavy tasks
    • Callback hell issue
    • Immature tooling
    • Bad dependency management

    Python

    Python is a universal programming language for various purposes, including web development. It has a large and active community, which means there are plenty of libraries and frameworks for Python developers. It is also a popular choice for machine learning and data analysis.

    Top Companies Using Python

    • Intel
    • IBM
    • NASA
    • Pixar
    • Netflix
    • Facebook
    • JP Morgan Chase
    • Spotify

    Pros of Python

    • Flexible and Extensible
    • Large Community
    • Extensive Libraries
    • Highly Scalable
    • Embeddable

    Cons of Python

    • Some issues with the design
    • Some security issues
    • High memory consumption
    • Complex multithreading
    • Slower than compiled language

    Ruby on Rails

    It's a web app framework that is written in Ruby. It follows the convention over the configuration philosophy, which can make the backend development procedure quicker and extra efficient. Ruby on Rails is particularly useful for quickly creating web applications, and small startups and large corporations widely use it.

    Top Companies Using Ruby on Rails

    • CrunchBase
    • Twitter
    • Etsy
    • Shopify
    • GitHub
    • Airbnb
    • Ask.fm
    • Fab

    Pros of Ruby on Rails

    • Time efficiency
    • Big and Active Community
    • A good number of helpful tools and libraries
    • Strong adherence to standards

    Cons of Ruby on Rails

    • Less flexible
    • Needs continuous evolvement
    • Less performance of boot time
    • Mistakes will be costly

    Express.js

    Express.js is a popular framework for Node.js. It is minimalistic and flexible, making it easy for developers to create and maintain web applications. Express.js is particularly useful for building RESTful APIs and can be combined with other Node.js libraries and frameworks.

    Top Companies Using Express.Js

    • Twitter
    • Accenture
    • Stack
    • Kevin
    • Client Platform
    • Intuit
    • Trustpilot
    • Pratilipi

    Pros of Express.js

    • Fast app development
    • Request handling for I/O
    • Open-source and big community
    • Easy integration of third-party services
    • Easy to learn

    Cons of Express.js

    • Event-driven nature (callbacks)
    • Code organization
    • Some error messages are not helpful
    • Not great on security

    Django

    Django, we all know, is a high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and spotless, pragmatic design. It is built on top of Python and is often used for web development in large organizations. It also offers a built-in admin interface that lets developers quickly create a backend for managing the application's data.

    Top Companies Using Django

    • Instagram
    • National Geographic
    • Mozilla
    • Spotify
    • Pinterest
    • Disqus
    • Bitbucket
    • Eventbrite

    Pros of Django

    • Implemented in Python
    • Better content management and better CDN
    • Fast processing
    • Rapid development
    • Scalable
    • Security

    Cons of Django

    • Django is Monolithic
    • Not best for smaller projects
    • It can lead to slow websites
    • Lack of conventions

    Final Words

    The industry is constantly changing, and backend and frontend developers must stay informed and adaptable to deliver high-quality products and services. You can enhance your development as a developer by staying current on the newest back and front web development trends and tools.

    Suppose you have a unique idea about web or mobile app development and seek a genuine and trustworthy partner.

    In that case, you can reach out to frontend development services and backend development services as per your needs.

    Remember to like, follow, comment, and share! Thanks for reading!

item.title: 让我们通过制作 RPG 来学习 Godot 4 — 第 12 部分：玩家射击和造成伤害 🤠 | Let’s Learn Godot 4 by Making an RPG — Part 12: Player Shooting & Dealing Damage 🤠
item.description:
如果你不能射击敌人，那么敌人有什么用呢？ 在这一部分中，我们将创建玩家可以朝他们面对的方向射击的子弹。 然后我们将添加一个动画，显示如果我们的子弹击中敌人，他们就会受到伤害。 如果子弹击中他们的生命值耗尽，他们也会死亡。 稍后，我们将提高玩家的 XP 并在敌人死亡时为他们添加战利品。 但现在，让我们系好安全带，因为这一部分将会很长！

您将在本部分中学到什么：

  • 如何使用AnimationPlayer节点。
  • 如何使用 RayCast2D 节点。
  • 如何使用调制值。
  • 如何使用 Time 类。

可生成的子弹

让我们首先创建当玩家射击或攻击时会产生的子弹。 该场景将类似于我们的 Pickups 场景的结构。 创建一个以 Area2D 节点作为根的新场景。 将此节点重命名为 Bullet 并将其保存在 Scenes 文件夹下。

它有一条警告消息，因为它没有形状。 让我们通过向其添加一个 CollisionShape2D 节点并使用 RectangleShape2D 作为其形状来解决此问题。

我们还需要查看我们的节点/项目符号。 我们的子弹将具有撞击动画，因此我们需要添加一个 AnimatedSprite2D 节点。

在 Inspector 面板中添加一个新的 SpriteFrames 资源，并在下面的 SpriteFrames 窗格中添加一个名为“impact”的新动画。 我们将用于子弹的 spritesheet 可以在 Assets > FX > Death Explosion.png 下找到。

水平方向我们计数 8 帧，垂直方向我们计数 1 帧，因此让我们相应地更改值以裁剪动画帧。 另外，仅选择动画的前三帧。

将 FPS 值更改为 10 并将其循环值关闭。

我们还需要向场景添加一个计时器节点并启用自动播放。 当子弹在未击中或撞击任何物体后需要“自毁”时，该计时器将发出timeout()信号。

最后，将脚本添加到场景中并将其保存在“脚本”文件夹下。

好的，现在我们可以开始讨论我们的 Bullet 场景需要做什么。 我们首先需要定义一些变量来设置子弹的速度、方向和伤害。 我们还需要再次引用我们的 Tilemap 节点，以便我们可以忽略与某些图层的碰撞，例如我们的水，它添加了碰撞，但它不应该阻止子弹。

 ###Bullets.gd extends Area2D #bullet variables var tilemap var speed = 80 var direction : Vector2 var damage

让我们在ready()函数中初始化tilemap节点，这样我们就可以摆脱它了！

 ###Bullets.gd #older code #initialize tilemap node func _ready(): tilemap = get_tree().root.get_node("Main/Map")

然后我们需要计算子弹射出后的位置。 如果我们的玩家移动，应该为每一帧重新计算这个位置。 因此，我们可以在内置的process()函数中计算它，以便它在每个帧移动期间更新其方向。 我们可以通过将其当前位置与其方向乘以当前帧捕获为增量的速度相加来计算该位置。

 ###Bullets.gd #older code #The position of the bullet must be recalculated at each frame func _process(delta): position = position + speed * delta * direction

当我们进行拾音器时，我们将 Area2D 的 body_entered() 信号连接到场景，以将拾音器添加到玩家的库存中。 我们想对我们的子弹场景做同样的事情，但这次我们将检查进入的身体是我们的敌人，以便我们可以伤害他们。 我们还想检查进入 Bullets 碰撞的物体是否是 Player 层或 TileMap 中的“Water”层，因为我们想忽略这些碰撞。

将 body_entered() 信号连接到您的 Bullet 脚本。 您将看到它在脚本末尾创建了一个新的“func _on_body_entered(body):”函数。 在这个新函数中，让我们实现上面设定的目标。


 #Collision detection for the bullet func _on_body_entered(body): # Ignore collision with Player if body.name == "Player": return # Ignore collision with Water if body.name == "Map": #water == Layer 0 if tilemap.get_layer_name(0): return # If the bullets hit an enemy, damage them if body.name.find("Enemy") >= 0: #todo: add damage/hit function to enemy scene pass

我们还无法对敌人造成伤害，因为他们还没有设置任何生命值变量或功能。 我们将在一分钟内完成此操作，但现在，让我们停止子弹移动，并将其动画更改为“撞击”（如果它没有击中任何东西）。 我们这样做是因为我们不希望随机的子弹在我们的场景中漂浮。

 ###Bullets.gd #older code #Collision detection for the bullet func _on_body_entered(body): # Ignore collision with Player if body.name == "Player": return # Ignore collision with Water if body.name == "Map": #water == Layer 0 if tilemap.get_layer_name(0): return # If the bullets hit an enemy, damage them if body.name.find("Enemy") >= 0: #todo: add damage/hit function to enemy scene pass # Stop the movement and explode direction = Vector2.ZERO $AnimatedSprite2D.play("impact")

我们还需要在子弹停止移动并停止播放“撞击”动画后将其从场景中删除。 我们之前已经在 Pickups 场景中完成了此操作，所以让我们继续将AnimationSprite2Danimation_finished信号连接到我们的 Bullet 脚本。


 ###Bullets.gd #older code #remove the bullet from the scene func _on_animated_sprite_2d_animation_finished(): if $AnimatedSprite2D.animation == "impact": get_tree().queue_delete(self)

最后，在 Bullet 场景中，将 Timer 节点的timeout()信号连接到 Bullet 脚本。 我们希望这个函数在两秒后没有击中任何东西后也播放“撞击”动画，因为播放这个动画将触发animation_finished信号，这将从我们的场景中删除子弹。


 ### Bullet.gd # older code #If the bullet does not hit anything, it will self-destruct in 2 seconds func _on_timer_timeout(): $AnimatedSprite2D.play("impact")

不要忘记将 Timer 节点的等待时间更改为 2，以便它在 2 秒后播放动画，而不是 1 秒。

球员射击

现在，我们已经完成了子弹场景，因为我们有了子弹运动和动画设置，并且能够从场景中删除子弹。 我们现在需要返回到 Player 场景，以便我们可以通过ui_attack输入发射子弹。

在您的玩家脚本中，让我们定义一些变量来设置子弹伤害、重新加载时间和子弹发射时间。 我们还需要创建子弹场景的实例，以便我们可以在玩家射击时在他们的前面生成它。

 ### Player.gd #older code #pickups enum Pickups { AMMO, STAMINA, HEALTH } var ammo_pickup = 12 var health_pickup = 0 var stamina_pickup = 0 # Bullet & attack variables var bullet_damage = 30 var bullet_reload_time = 1000 var rounds_per_second = 0.5 var bullet_scene = preload("res://Scenes/Bullet.tscn")

现在我们可以更改ui_attack *输入来生成子弹、计算重载并移除弹药。 当我们生成这些子弹时，我们需要考虑子弹的发射时间与重新加载时间。 我们希望我们的玩家在能够发射下一轮之前休息 1000 毫秒。 要获取 Godot 中的时间，我们可以使用Time 对象。 我们将使用 *.get_ticks_msec() 方法进行精确的时间计算。

首先，我们将检查我们当前的时间是否大于或等于我们的bullet_fired_time ，以及我们是否有弹药可以射击（确保您首先为您的玩家分配一些弹药）。

如果它更大，那就意味着我们可以发射一轮。 然后我们将is_attacking布尔值返回为 true 并播放我们的射击动画。 我们将通过将 reload_time 添加到当前时间来更新bullet_fired_time。 这意味着我们的玩家在下一轮开火之前将有 1000 毫秒的暂停。 最后，我们将更新我们的弹药拾取量。

 ### Player.gd #older code #plays attacking animation func _input(event): #input event for our attacking, ie our shooting if event.is_action_pressed("ui_attack"): #checks the current time as the amount of time passed in milliseconds since the engine started var now = Time.get_ticks_msec() #check if player can shoot if the reload time has passed and we have ammo if now >= bullet_fired_time and ammo_pickup > 0: #shooting anim is_attacking = true var animation = "attack_" + returned_direction(new_direction) $AnimatedSprite2D.play(animation) #bullet fired time time to current time bullet_fired_time = now + bullet_reload_time #reduce and signal ammo change ammo_pickup = ammo_pickup - 1 ammo_pickups_updated.emit(ammo_pickup) #using health consumables elif event.is_action_pressed("ui_consume_health"): #other code

我们将在func _on_animated_sprite_2d_animation_finished():函数中生成子弹，因为只有在射击动画播放后，我们才希望将子弹添加到主场景中。 我们必须创建 Bullet 场景的另一个实例，在其中更新其伤害、方向和位置，即玩家发射子弹时所面对的方向，以及玩家前方 4-5 个像素的位置（您不希望子弹从玩家内部射出，而是从枪的“枪管”射出）。 我们还可以让玩家朝鼠标指向的方向射击，而不是朝玩家面对的方向射击，所以如果您希望我向您展示如何实现这一点，请告诉我。

 ### Player.gd #older code #resets our attacking state back to false func _on_animated_sprite_2d_animation_finished(): is_attacking = false # Instantiate Bullet if $AnimatedSprite2D.animation.begins_with("attack_"): var bullet = bullet_scene.instantiate() bullet.damage = bullet_damage bullet.direction = new_direction.normalized() # Place it 4-5 pixels away in front of the player to simulate it coming from the guns barrel bullet.position = position + new_direction.normalized() * 4 get_tree().root.get_node("Main").add_child(bullet)

现在，如果您运行场景，按下 CTRL 发射子弹（ui_attack 输入操作）后，应该会生成一颗子弹。 对于子弹来说有点大，所以让我们改变它的大小！

在 Bullet 场景中，选择 AnimatedSprite2D 节点后，将其 Transform 属性下方的缩放值从 1 更改为 0.4。

另外，将子弹 FX 的第四帧添加到“冲击”动画中。 很抱歉现在添加它给您带来不便，我只是觉得最后一刻看起来更好！

现在，如果您运行场景，您的子弹应该小得多。 它还应该在 2 秒后或撞击另一个碰撞体时自毁。 现在我们需要添加让敌人受到子弹撞击伤害的功能。

敌人伤害

回到我们的子弹脚本，我添加了一个#todo ，因为我们仍然没有在敌人场景中设置伤害函数或健康变量。 我们现在就这么做。

在您的敌人脚本中，让我们设置其健康变量。 就像玩家一样，我们需要变量来存储其生命值、最大生命值和生命值再生，以及死亡时触发的信号。

 ### Enemy.gd # older variables #custom signals signal death #enemy stats var health = 100 var max_health = 100 var health_regen = 1

我们将在其_process(delta)函数中计算其健康再生，因为我们希望在每一帧计算它。 当我们计算 update_health 值时，我们已经在 Player 脚本中计算了这一点。

 ### Enemy.gd # older code func _process(delta): #regenerates our enemy's health health = min(health + health_regen * delta, max_health)

让我们继续创建我们的损伤函数。 这将是我们在玩家和子弹场景中调用的函数，用于在子弹/攻击撞击时对敌人造成伤害。 在此之前，我们还需要给敌人一些攻击变量。 我们可以从玩家脚本中复制攻击变量。

 ### Enemy.gd # older variables # Bullet & attack variables var bullet_damage = 50 var bullet_reload_time = 1000 var bullet_fired_time = 0.5 var bullet_scene = preload("res://Scenes/Bullet.tscn")

伤害函数将通过玩家或攻击者传递的子弹伤害来减少敌人的生命值。 如果他们的生命值大于零，敌人就会受到伤害。 如果它小于或等于零，敌人就会死亡。

 ### Enemy.gd # older code #will damage the enemy when they get hit func hit(damage): health -= damage if health > 0: #damage pass else: #death pass

我们将慢慢完成我们的伤害功能，所以让我们开始添加一个动画来表明我们的敌人已被击中。 为此，我们可以通过AnimationPlayer节点更改敌人的调制值。 调制值指的是节点的颜色，因此简单来说，我们将使用 AnimationPlayer 将敌人的颜色短暂变为红色，以便我们可以看到它们受到了损坏。 每当您需要为非精灵项目（例如标签或颜色）设置动画时，您都将使用此 AnimationPlayer 节点。 让我们将此节点添加到我们的敌人场景中。

我们将动画添加到编辑器底部“动画”面板中的“AnimationPlayer”节点。

要添加动画，请单击“动画”标签并说“新建”。 我们将这个新动画称为“损坏”。

您的新动画将分配长度“1”，因为您可以看到值从 0 到 1。让我们将此长度更改为 0.2，因为我们希望此损坏指示器非常短。 您可以通过按住 CTRL 并使用鼠标滚轮缩放来放大/缩小轨道。

我们希望此动画能够短暂更改 AnimatedSprite2D 节点的调制（颜色）值，这是我们可以在节点的检查器面板中更改的属性。 因此，我们需要添加一个新的Property Track。 单击“+ 添加轨道”并选择属性轨道。

将此轨道连接到您的 AnimatedSprite2D 节点，因为这是您想要设置动画的节点。

接下来，我们需要选择要更改的节点的属性。 我们需要调制值，因此从列表中选择它。

现在我们有了要更改的属性，我们需要将关键点插入轨道。 这些将是动画关键帧，定义动画的开始和/或结束点。 让我们插入两个键：一个位于 0 值（起点），另一个位于 0.2 值（终点）。 要插入密钥，只需右键单击曲目并选择“插入密钥”。

如果您单击立方体或键，检查器面板将打开，您将看到您可以在“值”下方的该帧处更改节点的调制值。

我们希望调制值从关键帧 0 处的红色变为关键帧 1 处的白色。为了使调制值变为红色，我们只需将其RGB值更改为 (255, 0, 0)。

我们还想更改动画的跟踪速率。 如果您现在运行动画，调制颜色将逐渐从红色变为白色。 相反，我们希望当达到 0.2 关键帧值时它立即从红色变为白色。 为此，我们可以更改其轨道速率，该速率位于我们轨道旁边的弯曲白线下方。

连续：更新每一帧的属性。

  • 离散：仅更新关键帧上的属性。

  • 触发器：仅更新关键帧或触发器上的属性。

    • 我们需要将跟踪速率从连续更改为离散。 它应该看起来像这样：

    现在，如果您运行此动画，它应该将敌人精灵的颜色短暂更改为红色，然后返回到其默认颜色。

    我们可以回到我们的伤害函数，现在如果敌人受到伤害就播放这个动画。

     ### Enemy.gd # older code #will damage the enemy when they get hit func hit(damage): health -= damage if health > 0: #damage $AnimationPlayer.play("damage") else: #death Pass

    让我们回到 Bullet 脚本中的#todo，并将其替换为我们刚刚创建的损坏函数。

     ### Bullet.gd # older code #Collision detection for the bullet func _on_body_entered(body): # Ignore collision with Player if body.name == "Player": return # Ignore collision with Water if body.name == "Map": #water == Layer 0 if tilemap.get_layer_name(0): return # If the bullets hit an enemy, damage them if body.name.find("Enemy") >= 0: body.hit(damage) # Stop the movement and explode direction = Vector2.ZERO $AnimatedSprite2D.play("impact")

    敌人死亡

    最后，在本节中，我们需要添加让敌人死亡的功能。 我们已经为此创建了信号，现在我们只需要更新敌人脚本中的现有代码来播放死亡动画并从场景树中删除敌人，以及更新我们的敌人生成器代码以连接到此信号并更新我们的敌人计数。

    让我们从死亡条件下的敌人脚本开始。 当我们的敌人死亡时，我们要做的第一件事就是停止处理其移动和方向的计时器。 我们还需要阻止process()函数重新生成敌人的生命值，因此我们将*set_process * 值设置为 false。

    我们还需要将 is_attacking 变量设置为 true，以便我们可以触发动画完成信号，以便我们可以在死亡动画播放后从场景中删除节点。 我们只是希望我们的敌人停下来，播放它的死亡动画，然后发出它已经死亡的信号，以便新的敌人可以产生。

     # Enemy.gd #will damage the enemy when they get hit func hit(damage): health -= damage if health > 0: #damage $AnimationPlayer.play("damage") else: #death #stop movement $Timer.stop() direction = Vector2.ZERO #stop health regeneration set_process(false) #trigger animation finished signal is_attacking = true #Finally, we play the death animation and emit the signal for the spawner. $AnimatedSprite2D.play("death") death.emit()

    这里剩下要做的就是在敌人死亡动画播放后将其从场景中移除。

     ### Enemy.gd # older code #resets our attacking state back to false func _on_animated_sprite_2d_animation_finished(): if $AnimatedSprite2D.animation == "spawn": $Timer.start() timer = 0 #Once the death animation has played, we can remove the enemy from the scene tree if $AnimatedSprite2D.animation == "death": get_tree().queue_delete(self) is_attacking = false

    现在我们需要转到 EnemySpawner 脚本来创建一个函数，该函数将在 Enemy 脚本发出死亡信号后从敌人计数中删除一个点。

     ### EnemySpawner.gd # older code #decrease the quantity of enemy by one func _on_enemy_death(): enemy_count = enemy_count - 1

    我们希望这个函数连接到我们的生成函数中的信号，以便我们的生成器知道敌人已被移除并且它应该生成一个新的敌人。

     ### EnemySpawner.gd # older code #enemy spawning func spawn_enemy(): var enemy = enemy_scene.instantiate() add_child(enemy) #We have to connect the signal from the enemy script. enemy.death.connect(_on_enemy_death) #spawns enemies on valid locations var valid_location = false while !valid_location: #keep looping to find a valid randomized spawn location on our maps x, y axis enemy.position.x = spawn_area.position.x + rng.randf_range(0, spawn_area.size.x) enemy.position.y = spawn_area.position.y + rng.randf_range(0, spawn_area.size.y) #set valid location to be enemies new position valid_location = valid_spawn_location(enemy.position) #spawn enemy with animation delay enemy.spawn()

    您的最终代码应该如下所示。

    因此，我们的玩家现在可以发射子弹，如果它击中敌人，就会对他们造成伤害。 三击后，它会杀死敌人并将其从场景中移走！

    故障排除：我的敌人保持或生成为红色！

    如果您注意到，当您启动游戏时，您的敌人会以红色生成，或者如果您射击它们，它们会保持红色，您只需重置动画并在敌人的read()函数中分配原始调制值。 当他们进入主场景时，这会将他们的颜色设置为精灵颜色。

     ### Enemy.gd # older code func _ready(): player = get_tree().root.get_node("Main/Player") rng.randomize() #reset modulate value so the enemy doesn't stay red $AnimatedSprite2D.modulate.r = 1 $AnimatedSprite2D.modulate.g = 1 $AnimatedSprite2D.modulate.b = 1 $AnimatedSprite2D.modulate.a = 1

    故障排除：我的敌人的死亡动画无法播放！

    如果您注意到敌人只是从场景中移除而没有播放其死亡动画，那是因为在敌人被移除之前动画播放得不够快。 要解决此问题，只需将动画代码移至死亡条件的顶部即可。

     ### Enemy.gd # older code func hit(damage): health -= damage if health > 0: #damage $AnimationPlayer.play("damage") else: #death #we play the death animation $AnimatedSprite2D.play("death") #stop movement $Timer.stop() direction = Vector2.ZERO #stop health regeneration set_process(false) #trigger animation finished signal is_attacking = true #we emit the signal for the spawner. death.emit()

    这就是这部分的内容。 这是一项相当大的工作，如果你做到了这一步，恭喜你坚持不懈！ 接下来，我们将在敌人死亡时生成战利品，然后添加功能，让他们攻击我们的玩家角色并对我们造成伤害！ 请记住保存您的游戏项目，我们下一部分见。

    教程发布

    其他部分的链接

    该教程系列共有 23 章。 我将在接下来的几周内以每日部分的形式发布所有章节。 您可以在此处找到本系列所有 23 个部分的教程链接的更新列表。

    完整教程

    如果您喜欢本系列或想跳过等待并访问该教程系列的离线完整版，您可以通过在Ko-fi 上购买离线小册子仅需 3 美元来支持我！ 😊 😊



    原文:

    What good is an enemy if you can’t shoot them? In this part, we’re going create bullets that our player can shoot in the direction that they’re facing. Then we’re going to add an animation that will show that our enemy is being damaged if our bullet hits them. If the bullets hit them enough for their health value to run out, they will also die. Later on, we will up our player’s XP and add loot to the enemy on their death. But for now, let’s buckle up because this part is going to be a long one!

    WHAT YOU WILL LEARN IN THIS PART:

    • How to use the AnimationPlayer node.
    • How to use the RayCast2D node.
    • How to work with modulate values.
    • How to work with the Time class.

    Spawnable Bullet

    Let’s start by creating the bullets that will spawn when our player shoots or attacks. This scene will be similar to our Pickups scene’s structure. Create a new scene with an Area2D node as its root. Rename this node as Bullet and save it under your Scenes folder.

    It has a warning message because it has no shape. Let’s fix this by adding a CollisionShape2D node to it with a RectangleShape2D as its shape.

    We also need to see our node/bullet. Our bullet will have an impact animation, so we need to add an AnimatedSprite2D node.

    Add a new SpriteFrames resource to it in the Inspector panel, and in your SpriteFrames pane below, let’s add a new animation called “impact”. The spritesheet we will use for our bullet can be found under Assets > FX > Death Explosion.png.

    Horizontally, we count 8 frames, and vertically we count 1 frame, so let’s change our values accordingly to crop out our animation frames. Also, select only the first three frames for your animation.

    Change the FPS value to 10 and turn its looping value to off.

    We also need to add a Timer node to our scene with autoplay enabled. This timer will emit its timeout() signal when the bullet needs to “self-destruct” after it’s not hit or impacted anything.

    Finally, add a script to your scene and save it under your Scripts folder.

    Okay, now we can start talking about what our Bullet scene needs to do. We first need to define a few variables which will set the bullet’s speed, direction, and damage. We also need to reference our Tilemap node again so that we can ignore the collision with certain layers, such as our water, which has collisions added to it, but it shouldn’t stop the bullet.

     ###Bullets.gd
 extends Area2D #bullet variables
 var tilemap var speed = 80 var direction : Vector2 var damage

    Let’s initialize our tilemap node in our ready() function just so that we can get it out of the way!

     ###Bullets.gd
 #older code
 #initialize tilemap node
 func _ready(): tilemap = get_tree().root.get_node("Main/Map")

    Then we need to calculate the position of our bullet after it’s been shot. This position should be re-calculated for each frame in case our player moves. Therefore we can calculate it in our built-in process() function so that it updates its direction during each frame movement. We can calculate this position by adding its current position by its direction times its speed at the current frame captured as delta.

     ###Bullets.gd
 #older code
 #The position of the bullet must be recalculated at each frame  func _process(delta): position = position + speed * delta * direction

    When we did our Pickups, we connected the Area2D’s body_entered() signal to our scene to add the pickups to our player’s inventory. We want to do the same for our Bullet scene, but this time we will check the body entered is our enemy so that we can injure them. We also want to check if the body entering our Bullets collision is the Player or “Water” layer from our TileMap because we want to ignore these collisions.

    Connect the body_entered() signal to your Bullet script. You will see that it creates a new ‘func _on_body_entered(body):’ function at the end of your script. Inside this new function, let’s accomplish the objectives we set above.


     #Collision detection for the bullet  func _on_body_entered(body): # Ignore collision with Player
 if body.name == "Player": return # Ignore collision with Water
 if body.name == "Map": #water == Layer 0
 if tilemap.get_layer_name(0): return # If the bullets hit an enemy, damage them
 if body.name.find("Enemy") >= 0: #todo: add damage/hit function to enemy scene
 pass

    We cannot damage the enemy yet because they do not have any health variables or functions set up. We will do that in a minute, but for now, let’s stop the bullet from moving and change its animation to “impact” if it does not hit anything. We’re doing this because we don’t want a random bullet just floating around in our scene.

     ###Bullets.gd
 #older code
 #Collision detection for the bullet  func _on_body_entered(body): # Ignore collision with Player
 if body.name == "Player": return # Ignore collision with Water
 if body.name == "Map": #water == Layer 0
 if tilemap.get_layer_name(0): return # If the bullets hit an enemy, damage them
 if body.name.find("Enemy") >= 0: #todo: add damage/hit function to enemy scene
 pass # Stop the movement and explode
 direction = Vector2.ZERO $AnimatedSprite2D.play("impact")

    We also need to delete the bullet from the scene after it stopped moving and stopped playing the “impact” animation. We’ve done this before in our Pickups scene, so let’s go ahead and connect our AnimationSprite2D animation_finished signal to our Bullet script.


     ###Bullets.gd
 #older code
 #remove the bullet from the scene
 func _on_animated_sprite_2d_animation_finished(): if $AnimatedSprite2D.animation == "impact": get_tree().queue_delete(self)

    Finally, in your Bullet scene, connect your Timer node’s timeout() signal to your Bullet script. We want this function to also play the “impact” animation after it’s not hit anything after two seconds because playing this animation will trigger the animation_finished signal, which will delete the bullet from our scene.


     ### Bullet.gd
 # older code
 #If the bullet does not hit anything, it will self-destruct in 2 seconds
 func _on_timer_timeout(): $AnimatedSprite2D.play("impact")

    Don’t forget to change your Timer node’s wait time to 2 so that it plays the animation after 2 seconds and not 1.

    Player Shooting

    For now, we are done with our Bullet scene since we have our bullet movement and animation setup, plus the ability to remove the bullet from a scene. We need to now go back to our Player scene so that we can fire off bullets via our ui_attack input.

    In your player script, let’s define some variables that would set the bullet damage, reload time, and the bullet fired time. We also need to create an instance of our bullet scene so that we can spawn it at our player’s front when they shoot.

     ### Player.gd
 #older code
 #pickups
 enum Pickups { AMMO, STAMINA, HEALTH } var ammo_pickup = 12 var health_pickup = 0 var stamina_pickup = 0 # Bullet & attack variables
 var bullet_damage = 30 var bullet_reload_time = 1000 var rounds_per_second = 0.5 var bullet_scene = preload("res://Scenes/Bullet.tscn")

    Now we can change our ui_attack *input to spawn bullets, calculate the reload, and remove ammo. When we spawn these bullets, we need to take into consideration the time the bullet was fired vs. the reload time. We want our player to take a 1000-ms break before being able to fire off the next round. To get the time in Godot, we can use the Time object. The Time singleton allows converting time between various formats and also getting time information from the system. We will use the method *.get_ticks_msec() for precise time calculation.

    First, we will check if our current time is bigger or equal to our bullet_fired_time AND if we have ammo to shoot (make sure you assign some ammo to your player first).

    If it is bigger, that means we can fire off a round. We will then return our is_attacking boolean as true and play our shooting animation. We will update our bullet_fired_time by adding our reload_time to our current time. This means our player will have a 1000-ms pause before they fire off the next round. Finally, we will update our ammo pickup amount.

     ### Player.gd
 #older code
 #plays attacking animation
 func _input(event): #input event for our attacking, i.e. our shooting
 if event.is_action_pressed("ui_attack"): #checks the current time as the amount of time passed in milliseconds since the engine started
 var now = Time.get_ticks_msec() #check if player can shoot if the reload time has passed and we have ammo
 if now >= bullet_fired_time and ammo_pickup > 0: #shooting anim
 is_attacking = true var animation = "attack_" + returned_direction(new_direction) $AnimatedSprite2D.play(animation) #bullet fired time time to current time
 bullet_fired_time = now + bullet_reload_time #reduce and signal ammo change
 ammo_pickup = ammo_pickup - 1 ammo_pickups_updated.emit(ammo_pickup) #using health consumables
 elif event.is_action_pressed("ui_consume_health"): #other code

    We will spawn our bullet in our func _on_animated_sprite_2d_animation_finished(): function, because only after the shooting animation has played do we want our bullet to be added to our Main scene. We’ll have to create another instance of our Bullet scene, where we will update its damage, direction, and position as the direction the player was facing when they fired off the round, and the position 4–5 pixels in front of the player (you don’t want the bullet to come from inside of your player, but instead from the gun’s “barrel”). We can also have the player shoot in the direction that our mouse is pointing at instead of in the direction that our player is facing, so just let me know if you want me to show you how to implement that.

     ### Player.gd
 #older code
 #resets our attacking state back to false
 func _on_animated_sprite_2d_animation_finished(): is_attacking = false # Instantiate Bullet
 if $AnimatedSprite2D.animation.begins_with("attack_"): var bullet = bullet_scene.instantiate() bullet.damage = bullet_damage bullet.direction = new_direction.normalized() # Place it 4-5 pixels away in front of the player to simulate it coming from the guns barrel
 bullet.position = position + new_direction.normalized() * 4 get_tree().root.get_node("Main").add_child(bullet)

    Now if you run your scene, a bullet should spawn after you press CTRL to fire off a bullet (ui_attack input action). It’s a bit big for a bullet, so let’s change its size!

    In your Bullet scene, with your AnimatedSprite2D node selected, change its scale value from 1 to 0.4 underneath its Transform property.

    Also, add that fourth frame of the bullet FX to your “impact” animation. Sorry for the inconvenience of adding it now, I just thought it looked better at the last minute!

    Now if you run your scene, your bullet should be much smaller. It should also self-destruct after 2 seconds, or when it hits another collision body. Now we need to add the functionality for our enemy to be damaged by a bullet impact.

    Enemy Damage

    Back in our Bullet script, I added a #todo since we still do not have a damage function or health variables set up in our Enemy scene. We will do that now.

    In your Enemy script, let’s set up its health variables. Just like the player, we need variables to store its health, max health, and health regeneration, as well as a signal to fire off when it dies.

     ### Enemy.gd
 # older variables
 #custom signals
 signal death #enemy stats
 var health = 100 var max_health = 100 var health_regen = 1

    We will calculate its health regeneration in its _process(delta) function since we want to calculate it at each frame. We’ve already calculated this in the Player script when we did its updated_health value.

     ### Enemy.gd
 # older code
 func _process(delta): #regenerates our enemy's health
 health = min(health + health_regen * delta, max_health)

    Let’s also go ahead and start creating our damage function. This will be the function that we call in our Player and Bullet scenes to damage the enemy upon bullet/attack impact. Before we do that, we need to also give the enemy some attack variables. We can just copy over the attack variables from our Player script.

     ### Enemy.gd
 # older variables
 # Bullet & attack variables
 var bullet_damage = 50 var bullet_reload_time = 1000 var bullet_fired_time = 0.5 var bullet_scene = preload("res://Scenes/Bullet.tscn")

    The damage function will decrease our Enemy’s health by the bullet damage passed in by the Player or attacker. If their health is more than zero, the enemy will be damaged. If it’s less than or equal to zero, the enemy will die.

     ### Enemy.gd
 # older code
 #will damage the enemy when they get hit
 func hit(damage): health -= damage if health > 0: #damage
 pass else: #death
 pass

    We will slowly make our way to completing our damage function, so let’s get started by adding an animation that would indicate that our Enemy has been hit. For this, we can change the enemy’s modulate value via the AnimationPlayer node. The modulate value refers to the node’s color, so in simple terms, we will use the AnimationPlayer to briefly turn our enemy’s color red so that we can see that they are damaged. You will use this AnimationPlayer node whenever you need to animate non-sprite items, such as labels or colors. Let’s add this node to our Enemy scene.

    We add animations to the AnimationPlayer node in the Animations panel at the bottom of the editor.

    To add an animation, click on the “Animation” label and say new. Let’s call this new animation “damage”.

    Your new animation will have a length of “1” assigned to it as you can see the values run from 0 to 1. Let’s change this length to 0.2 because we want this damage indicator to be very short. You can zoom in/out on your track by holding down CTRL and zooming with your mouse wheel.

    We want this animation to briefly change our AnimatedSprite2D node’s modulate (color) value, which is a property that we can change in the node’s Inspector panel. Therefore, we need to add a new Property Track. Click on “+ Add Track” and select Property Track.

    Connect this track to your AnimatedSprite2D node, since this is the node you want to animate.

    Next, we need to choose the property of the node that we want to change. We want the modulate value, so choose it from the list.

    Now that we have the property that we want to change, we need to insert keys to the track. These will be the animation keyframes, which define the starting and/or ending point of our animation. Let’s insert two keys: one on our 0 value (starting point), and one on our 0.2 value (ending point). To insert a key, just right-click on your track and select “Insert Key”.

    If you click on the cubes or keys, the Inspector panel will open, and you will see that you can change the modulate value of our node at that frame underneath “Value”.

    We want our modulate value to go from red at keyframe 0 to white at keyframe 1. To make our modulate value red, we can just change its RGB values to (255, 0, 0).

    We also want to change the track rate of our animation. If you were to run the animation now, the modulate color would gradually change from red to white. Instead, we want it to change instantly from red to white when the 0.2 keyframe value is reached. To do this we can change its track rate, which is found next to our track under the curved white line.

    Godot has three options for our track rate:

    • Continuous: Update the property on each frame.

    • Discrete: Only update the property on keyframes.

    • Trigger: Only update the property on keyframes or triggers.

    We need to change our track rate from continuous to discrete. It should look like this:

    Now if you run this animation, it should briefly change your enemy sprite’s color to red and then back to its default color.

    We can go back to our damage function and now play this animation if the enemy gets damaged.

     ### Enemy.gd
 # older code
 #will damage the enemy when they get hit
 func hit(damage): health -= damage if health > 0: #damage
 $AnimationPlayer.play("damage") else: #death
 Pass

    Let’s go back to our #todo in our Bullet script and replace it with the damage function that we just created.

     ### Bullet.gd
 # older code
 #Collision detection for the bullet  func _on_body_entered(body): # Ignore collision with Player
 if body.name == "Player": return # Ignore collision with Water
 if body.name == "Map": #water == Layer 0
 if tilemap.get_layer_name(0): return # If the bullets hit an enemy, damage them
 if body.name.find("Enemy") >= 0: body.hit(damage) # Stop the movement and explode
 direction = Vector2.ZERO $AnimatedSprite2D.play("impact")

    Enemy Death

    Lastly for this section, we need to add the functionality for our enemy to die. We already created our signal for this, now we just need to update our existing code in our Enemy script to play our death animation and remove the enemy from the scene tree, as well as update our Enemy Spawner code to connect to this signal and update our enemy count.

    Let’s start in our Enemy script underneath our death conditional. When our enemy dies, the first thing we do is stop the timer that handles its movement and direction. We also need to stop our process() function from regenerating our enemy’s health value, so we will set the *set_process* value to false.

    We also need to set our is_attacking variable equal to true so that we can trigger our animation finished signal so that we can remove our node from our scene after our death animation plays. We simply want our enemy to stop, play its death animation, and then signal that it has died so that a new enemy can spawn.

     # Enemy.gd
 #will damage the enemy when they get hit
 func hit(damage): health -= damage if health > 0: #damage
 $AnimationPlayer.play("damage") else: #death
 #stop movement
 $Timer.stop() direction = Vector2.ZERO #stop health regeneration
 set_process(false) #trigger animation finished signal
 is_attacking = true #Finally, we play the death animation and emit the signal for the spawner.
 $AnimatedSprite2D.play("death") death.emit()

    All that’s left to do here is to remove the enemy from the scene after their death animation has played.

     ### Enemy.gd
 # older code
 #resets our attacking state back to false
 func _on_animated_sprite_2d_animation_finished(): if $AnimatedSprite2D.animation == "spawn": $Timer.start() timer = 0 #Once the death animation has played, we can remove the enemy from the scene tree
 if $AnimatedSprite2D.animation == "death": get_tree().queue_delete(self) is_attacking = false

    Now we need to go to our EnemySpawner script to create a function that will remove a point from our enemy count after the death signal from our Enemy script has been emitted.

     ### EnemySpawner.gd
 # older code
 #decrease the quantity of enemy by one
 func _on_enemy_death(): enemy_count = enemy_count - 1

    We want this function to be connected to our signal in our spawn function so that our spawner knows that an enemy has been removed and it should spawn a new one.

     ### EnemySpawner.gd
 # older code
 #enemy spawning  func spawn_enemy(): var enemy = enemy_scene.instantiate() add_child(enemy) #We have to connect the signal from the enemy script.
 enemy.death.connect(_on_enemy_death) #spawns enemies on valid locations
 var valid_location = false while !valid_location: #keep looping to find a valid randomized spawn location on our maps x, y axis  enemy.position.x = spawn_area.position.x + rng.randf_range(0, spawn_area.size.x) enemy.position.y = spawn_area.position.y + rng.randf_range(0, spawn_area.size.y) #set valid location to be enemies new position
 valid_location = valid_spawn_location(enemy.position) #spawn enemy with animation delay
 enemy.spawn()

    Your final code should look like this.

    And so, our Player can now shoot a bullet and if it hits an enemy it damages them. After three hits, it kills the enemy and removes them from the scene!

    Troubleshooting: My Enemy Stays or Spawns as RED!

    If you’ve noticed that when you launch your game, your enemy spawns as a red color, or if you shoot them they stay red, you simply need to reset the animation and assign the original modulate value in your Enemy’s ready() function. This will set their color to be their sprite color when they enter the Main scene.

     ### Enemy.gd
 # older code
 func _ready(): player = get_tree().root.get_node("Main/Player") rng.randomize() #reset modulate value so the enemy doesn't stay red
 $AnimatedSprite2D.modulate.r = 1 $AnimatedSprite2D.modulate.g = 1 $AnimatedSprite2D.modulate.b = 1 $AnimatedSprite2D.modulate.a = 1

    Troubleshooting: My Enemy’s Death Animation Doesn’t Play!

    If you’ve noticed that your enemy just gets removed from the scene without them playing its death animation, it’s because the animation is not playing quickly enough before the enemy gets removed. To fix this, simply move your animation code to the top of your death conditional.

     ### Enemy.gd
 # older code
 func hit(damage): health -= damage if health > 0: #damage
 $AnimationPlayer.play("damage") else: #death
 #we play the death animation
 $AnimatedSprite2D.play("death") #stop movement
 $Timer.stop() direction = Vector2.ZERO #stop health regeneration
 set_process(false) #trigger animation finished signal
 is_attacking = true #we emit the signal for the spawner.  death.emit()

    And that’s it for this part. It was quite a lot of work, and if you made it this far, congratulations on being persistent! Next up we’re going to spawn loot upon our enemy’s death, and then we’ll add the functionality for them to attack our player character and deal damage to us! Remember to save your game project, and I’ll see you in the next part.

    TUTORIAL RELEASE

    LINKS TO OTHER PARTS

    The tutorial series has 23 chapters. I’ll be releasing all of the chapters in sectional daily parts over the next couple of weeks. You can find the updated list of the tutorial links for all 23 parts in this series here.

    FULL TUTORIAL

    If you like this series or want to skip the wait and access the offline, full version of the tutorial series, you can support me by buying the offline booklet for just $3 on Ko-fi!😊
item.author: christinec_dev

item.title: 成为 Web 开发人员的完整指南：第 3 部分 | The Complete Guide to Becoming a Web Developer: Part 3
item.description:
各位网络开发爱好者，大家好！ 如果您曾经构建过网站，那么您就已经与文档对象模型 (DOM) 进行了交互。 但 DOM 到底是什么？为什么它在 Web 开发中如此重要？ 系好安全带，因为我们即将深入探索 DOM 的迷人世界！

如果您是新来的，您可以从第 1 部分和第 2 部分开始。

  • 介绍

    它是一个编程接口，可以让您的网页变得栩栩如生、与用户交互并动态变化。 当您加载网页时，浏览器会创建该页面的 DOM，它本质上是网页结构的面向对象表示。

    将 DOM 视为树状结构，其中每个分支都以一个节点结束，每个节点都包含对象。 这些对象是网页的一部分，如 HTML 标签、文本内容、链接、图像等。DOM 允许 JavaScript 访问和操作这些对象、更改它们的属性、对事件做出反应，甚至创建新的元素和属性。

    了解 DOM 是 Web 开发的基础，因为它是 HTML/CSS 和 JavaScript 之间的桥梁。 如果没有 DOM，您的 JavaScript 代码将无法与网页的内容和结构进行交互。

    第 1 部分：理解 DOM

    欢迎来到我们深入探讨 DOM 的第一部分！ 现在我们已经介绍了基础知识，让我们卷起袖子深入了解 DOM 是什么、它如何工作以及它在 JavaScript 中的作用。

    什么是 DOM？

    文档对象模型（DOM）是 HTML 和 XML 文档的编程接口。 它以一种易于 JavaScript 等编程语言交互的方式表示文档的结构。 这些节点是表示文档部分的对象，例如元素、属性和文本。

    想象一下家谱。 祖父母在顶部（根节点），他们的孩子在他们下面（子节点），他们的孙子在他们下面（叶节点）。 类似地，在 DOM 树中， document对象是根节点， <body>和<head>等元素是子节点，其中的元素和文本是叶节点。

    当您加载网页时，浏览器会获取 HTML 并对其进行解析，从而创建 DOM。 该 DOM 不是作为字符串存储在浏览器的内存中，而是作为一组具有属性和方法的互连对象。

    我们来看一个简单的 HTML 文档：

     <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head> <title>My Web Page</title> </head> <body> <h1>Welcome to my web page!</h1> <p>This is a paragraph.</p> </body> </html>

    另一方面，浏览器会创建一个看起来像这样的 DOM：

     document |-- html |-- head |-- title |-- "My Web Page" |-- body |-- h1 |-- "Welcome to my web page!" |-- p |-- "This is a paragraph."

    它可以访问任何节点、更改其内容、应用样式、添加或删除节点等。 例如，如果您想更改<h1>标记内的文本，或者可以选择<body>节点并向其添加新的子节点。 您可以做很多事情，并使用 JavaScript 在 DOM 中选择这些节点并操作其内容。

    它允许 JavaScript 与 HTML 交互，动态更改文档的结构、内容和样式。

    如果没有 DOM，您的 JavaScript 代码将无法访问或操作 HTML。 您将无法响应用户事件、动态更新内容或创建交互式 Web 体验。

    换句话说，DOM 可以将静态 HTML 文档转变为动态的交互式网页。 这是您使用过的每个由 JavaScript 驱动的网站或网络应用程序背后的魔力。

    第 2 部分：选择元素

    好吧，既然我们已经对 DOM 有了深入的了解，那么让我们亲自动手并开始与它进行交互。 查询选择器

    它就像一把用于 DOM 选择的瑞士军刀。 您可以使用它通过标签名称、类、ID、属性等来选择元素。

    它的工作原理如下：

     let element = document.querySelector(selector);

    如果未找到匹配项，则返回null 。

    假设我们有以下 HTML：

     <div class="container"> <h1 id="title">Hello, World!</h1> <p class="text">Welcome to my web page.</p> </div>

    以下是如何使用querySelector选择上述元素：

     let title = document.querySelector('#title'); // selects the element with the ID "title" let text = document.querySelector('.text'); // selects the element with the class "text" let div = document.querySelector('div'); // selects the first <div> element

    查询选择器全部

    使用方法如下：

     let elements = document.querySelectorAll(selector);

    同样， selector是一个字符串，包含一个或多个以逗号分隔的 CSS 选择器。 如果没有找到匹配项，则返回一个空的 NodeList。

    使用与之前相同的 HTML，以下是如何使用querySelectorAll ：

     let divs = document.querySelectorAll('div'); // selects all <div> elements let elements = document.querySelectorAll('.text, #title'); // selects all elements with the class "text" and the ID "title"

    请记住， querySelectorAll返回一个 NodeList，而不是一个数组。 虽然您可以在 NodeList 上使用某些数组方法（例如forEach ），但其他方法（例如map和filter ）不可用。 如果需要使用这些方法，可以使用Array.from()将 NodeList 转换为数组。

    现在你就拥有了！ 您现在知道如何使用querySelector和querySelectorAll在 DOM 中选择元素。 这些方法非常强大且用途广泛，它们是所有 DOM 操作的基础。 因此，继续吧，开始选择元素并使用它们。

    第 3 节：DOM 最重要的属性和方法

    欢迎来到 DOM 操作的核心：属性和方法。 现在我们知道如何选择元素，是时候学习如何与它们交互了。 在本节中，我们将探讨允许我们读取、更改、添加和删除元素及其内容的各种属性和方法。 让我们深入了解吧！

    类列表

    此属性对于在元素上添加、删除和切换 CSS 类非常有用。

     let element = document.querySelector('#myElement'); element.classList.add('new-class'); // adds 'new-class' to the element element.classList.remove('new-class'); // removes 'new-class' from the element element.classList.toggle('new-class'); // toggles 'new-class'

    删除子对象（）和删除（）

    第 4 节：JavaScript 中的事件处理和最常见事件

    欢迎来到 JavaScript 事件处理的激动人心的世界！ 这是您的网页真正变得栩栩如生的地方，实时响应用户交互。 从简单的点击到复杂的拖放，JavaScript 事件是交互式 Web 体验的核心。 让我们深入了解吧！

    什么是事件处理？

    在 JavaScript 中，事件是发生某事的信号。 这可以是用户操作，例如单击按钮或提交表单，也可以是浏览器操作，例如加载页面或调整窗口大小。

    事件处理是在响应这些事件的元素上设置侦听器的过程。 当事件发生时，侦听器执行 JavaScript 函数，称为事件处理程序。

    事件处理在 JavaScript 中至关重要，因为它允许您的网页响应用户交互并创建动态的交互式体验。

    常见 JavaScript 事件

    让我们看一下一些常见的 JavaScript 事件以及如何处理它们。

    点击次数

    当用户单击某个元素时，将触发click事件。

     let button = document.querySelector('button'); button.addEventListener('click', function() { console.log('Button clicked!'); });

    拖放

    悬停

    当mouseover指针进入和离开元素时，将分别触发 mouseover 和mouseout事件。

     let element = document.querySelector('#myElement'); element.addEventListener('mouseover', function() { element.style.backgroundColor = 'yellow'; }); element.addEventListener('mouseout', function() { element.style.backgroundColor = ''; });

    当元素的滚动条滚动时，将触发scroll事件。

     window.addEventListener('scroll', function() { console.log('Scrolling!'); });

    提交表单时会触发submit事件。

     let form = document.querySelector('form'); form.addEventListener('submit', function(event) { event.preventDefault(); // prevent form from submitting normally console.log('Form submitted!'); });

    按键

    当用户按下或释放某个键时，将触发keydown 、 keypress和keyup事件。

     window.addEventListener('keydown', function(event) { console.log('Key pressed: ' + event.key); });

    聚焦/模糊

    当元素获得或失去焦点时， focus和blur事件就会触发。

     let input = document.querySelector('input'); input.addEventListener('focus', function() { console.log('Input focused!'); }); input.addEventListener('blur', function() { console.log('Input blurred!'); });

    双击

    双击元素时会触发dblclick事件。

     let button = document.querySelector('button'); button.addEventListener('dblclick',

    第 5 节：异步 JavaScript 和 DOM

    当我们深入研究 DOM 即将结束时，让我们花点时间展望一下未来。 具体来说，我们来谈谈异步 JavaScript 以及它如何与 DOM 交互。 这是一个广阔的主题，值得单独撰写一系列文章，但我们将在这里简要介绍一下，以便让您了解即将发生的事情。

    在 JavaScript 中，大多数操作都是同步的，这意味着它们会阻止进一步执行，直到完成为止。 但是，某些操作（例如从服务器获取数据或设置计时器）是异步的。 它们现在启动，但稍后完成，从而允许其余代码同时继续运行。

    它允许您的网页即使在执行耗时的任务（例如加载数据或图像）时也能保持交互。

    下面是异步 JavaScript 的一个简单示例：

     console.log('Fetching data...'); fetch('https://api.example.com/data') .then(response => response.json()) .then(data => { console.log('Data fetched!'); // Now you can use the data to update the DOM }); console.log('This will run before the data is fetched!');

    在此示例中， fetch函数返回一个 Promise，它是未来操作结果的占位符。 这只是异步 JavaScript 的冰山一角。 还有很多东西需要学习，包括回调、Promises、async/await 等等。 但别担心，我们将在以后的文章中介绍所有这些内容以及更多内容。 现在，只需知道异步 JavaScript 是创建动态、交互式 Web 体验的强大工具，并且它在 DOM 操作中发挥着至关重要的作用。

    请继续关注更多内容，并继续练习您到目前为止所学到的知识。 您正在成为 DOM 操作大师！

    第 6 节：DOM 操作中的常见陷阱

    好的，到目前为止我们已经介绍了很多内容。 我们已经了解了 DOM 是什么、如何选择元素以及如何使用属性和方法来操作这些元素。 现在，是时候讨论 DOM 操作中的一些常见陷阱了。 即使是经验丰富的开发人员也可能会陷入这些陷阱，所以让我们来了解一下它们并学习如何避免它们。

    陷阱 1：不等待 DOM 加载

    最常见的错误之一是尝试在 DOM 完全加载之前对其进行操作。 如果您的 JavaScript 在解析 HTML 之前运行，它将无法找到它应该与之交互的元素。

    为了避免这种情况，您可以使用DOMContentLoaded事件，该事件在初始 HTML 文档完全加载和解析后触发，而无需等待样式表、图像和子框架完成加载。

     document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() { // Your code here });

    陷阱 2：过度使用innerHTML

    虽然innerHTML是一个强大的属性，允许您获取或设置元素的HTML 内容，但它可能是性能问题和安全漏洞的根源。

    每次使用innerHTML时，浏览器都必须将新的HTML字符串解析为DOM树，这对于大量的HTML来说可能会很慢。 如果您不小心处理用户输入，它还会使您遭受跨站点脚本 (XSS) 攻击。

    最佳实践是，在处理文本时使用textContent或innerText ，在创建新元素时使用createElement 、 appendChild和其他 DOM 方法。

    陷阱 3：忘记 NodeList 不是数组

    当您使用querySelectorAll时，它会返回一个 NodeList，它是一个类似数组的对象，但不是实际的数组。 当您尝试在 NodeList 上使用数组方法（例如map 、 filter或reduce并获得 TypeError 时，这可能会导致混乱。

    为了避免这种情况，您可以使用Array.from()或扩展运算符 ( ... ) 将 NodeList 转换为数组。

     let elements = document.querySelectorAll('div'); let elementsArray = Array.from(elements); // or [...elements]

    陷阱 4：没有正确处理事件传播

    如果您不小心，当您与子元素交互时，父元素上的事件处理程序可能会触发。

    为了防止这种情况，您可以在事件处理程序中使用event.stopPropagation() 。 但是，请谨慎使用它，因为它会使您的代码更难以调试和理解。

     element.addEventListener('click', function(event) { event.stopPropagation(); // Your code here });

    请记住，每个人都会犯错误，这就是我们学习的方式。 关键是要了解这些常见的陷阱并学习如何避免它们。 快乐编码！

    第 7 节：构建一个简单的待办事项列表项目

    好吧，是时候将所有理论付诸实践了。 我们将构建一个简单的项目来演示本文中介绍的所有概念。 我们将创建一个动态的、交互式的待办事项列表，用户可以在其中添加任务、将其标记为已完成以及删除它们。 让我们开始吧！

    第 1 步：设置 HTML

    首先，让我们用 HTML 设置待办事项列表的基本结构。 我们需要一个用于添加新任务的输入字段、一个用于提交输入的按钮以及一个用于显示任务的无序列表。

     <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head> <title>To-Do List</title> </head> <body> <input type="text" id="taskInput" placeholder="New task"> <button id="addTaskButton">Add Task</button> <ul id="taskList"></ul> </body> </html>

    第2步：添加任务

    接下来，让我们使用 JavaScript 将任务添加到列表中。 我们将选择输入字段和按钮，并在按钮上设置一个事件侦听器，每当单击该按钮时，该事件侦听器都会将新列表项添加到任务列表中。

     let taskInput = document.querySelector('#taskInput'); let addTaskButton = document.querySelector('#addTaskButton'); let taskList = document.querySelector('#taskList'); addTaskButton.addEventListener('click', function() { let task = taskInput.value; // get the input value (the new task) let listItem = document.createElement('li'); // create a new list item listItem.textContent = task; // set the text content of the list item taskList.appendChild(listItem); // append the list item to the task list taskInput.value = ''; // clear the input field });

    步骤 3：将任务标记为已完成

    现在，让我们添加通过单击任务将其标记为已完成的功能。 我们将在任务列表上设置一个事件侦听器，检查单击事件的目标是否是列表项，如果是，则切换其上的“已完成”类。

     taskList.addEventListener('click', function(event) { if (event.target.tagName === 'LI') { event.target.classList.toggle('completed'); } });

    第 4 步：删除任务

    最后，让我们允许用户从列表中删除任务。 我们将为每个任务添加一个“删除”按钮，并在任务列表上设置一个事件侦听器，当单击“删除”按钮时删除任务。

     addTaskButton.addEventListener('click', function() { // ...previous code... let removeButton = document.createElement('button'); removeButton.textContent = 'Remove'; listItem.appendChild(removeButton); }); taskList.addEventListener('click', function(event) { if (event.target.tagName === 'BUTTON') { let task = event.target.parentElement; taskList.removeChild(task); } });

    第 5 步：添加一些样式

    body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; padding: 20px; } #taskInput, #addTaskButton { margin: 10px 0; } #taskList { list-style-type: none; padding: 0; } #taskList li { background-color: #f9f9f9; border: 1px solid #ddd; margin-bottom: 10px; padding: 10px 20px; display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; } #taskList li.completed { text-decoration: line-through; } button { background-color: #ff6347; color: white; border: none; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; } button:hover { background-color: #ee3120; }

    现在你就拥有了！ 您已经使用本文介绍的 DOM 操作技术构建了一个动态、交互式待办事项列表。 这只是一个简单的例子，但可能性是无限的。 继续练习，继续尝试，最重要的是，继续享受乐趣！ 快乐编码！

    元素的 ID（ taskInput 、 addTaskButton 、 taskList ）应该完全匹配。

  • 这确保了 JavaScript 代码在 HTML 完全加载后执行，因此它可以访问 DOM 中的元素。
  • 浏览器控制台：检查浏览器开发人员工具中的控制台是否有任何错误消息。 这些消息通常可以提供有关问题所在的线索。
  • 结论

    现在你就拥有了！ 我们已经穿越了文档对象模型 (DOM) 的迷人世界，探索了它的角落和缝隙，并揭开了它的秘密。 我们学习了如何选择元素、使用各种属性和方法操作它们、处理事件，甚至初步了解了异步 JavaScript 的领域。

    我们讨论了 DOM 操作中的常见陷阱以及如何避免它们，并且通过构建动态、交互式待办事项列表来测试所有新发现的知识。 在此过程中，我们看到了 JavaScript 如何为静态 HTML 注入活力，将其转变为充满活力的交互式体验。

    但请记住，这只是一个开始。 所以不要停在这里。 继续练习，继续尝试，最重要的是，继续享受乐趣。 尝试将新功能添加到待办事项列表中，或从头开始一个新项目。 您编写的代码越多，您就会对这些概念和技术越熟悉。

    请继续关注未来的文章，我们将更深入地探讨异步 JavaScript 和其他高级主题。 旅程永无止境，您迈出的每一步都让您离成为 Web 开发大师更近了一步。

    其他资源

    当我们结束 DOM 之旅时，您可能想知道下一步该去哪里。 为了帮助您继续学习之旅，我强烈推荐以下一些在线资源：

    1. 他们的JavaScript 和 DOM 操作指南是深入研究我们所讨论的主题的好地方。
    2. 他们的JavaScript HTML DOM教程对于初学者来说是一个很好的资源。
    3. 它可以免费在线阅读。
    4. 他们的JavaScript 算法和数据结构认证包括有关基本数据结构的部分，可以帮助您了解如何在 JavaScript 中使用数组和对象，这对于 DOM 操作至关重要。
    5. 请记住，掌握 Web 开发的关键是实践。 不要只是阅读这些概念——应用它们。 构建项目、解决问题，并且不要害怕犯错误。 这就是你学习的方式。

      快乐编码！



原文:

Hello there, web development enthusiasts! If you've ever built a website, you've interacted with the Document Object Model (DOM). But what exactly is the DOM, and why is it so crucial in web development? Buckle up, because we're about to dive deep into the fascinating world of the DOM!

If you're new here you can start with part 1 and part 2.

  • Introduction
  • Section 1: Understanding the DOM
    • What is the DOM?
    • How Does the DOM Work?
    • The Role of the DOM in JavaScript
  • Section 2: Selecting Elements
    • querySelector
    • querySelectorAll
  • Section 3: DOM Most Important Properties & Methods
    • classList
    • getAttribute() and setAttribute()
    • appendChild(), append(), and prepend()
    • removeChild() and remove()
    • createElement, innerText, textContent, and innerHTML
    • value, parentElement, children, nextSibling, previousSibling, and style
  • Section 4: Event Handling and Most Common Events in JavaScript
  • Section 5: Asynchronous JavaScript and the DOM
  • Section 6: Common Pitfalls in DOM Manipulation
  • Section 7: Building a Simple To-Do List Project
  • Conclusion
  • Additional Resources

Introduction

The DOM is like the backbone of your web page. It's a programming interface that allows your web page to come to life, interact with users, and change dynamically. When you load a web page, the browser creates the DOM of that page, which is essentially an object-oriented representation of the web page's structure.

Think of the DOM as a tree-like structure where each branch ends in a node, and each node contains objects. These objects are parts of your webpage like HTML tags, text content, links, images, etc. The DOM allows JavaScript to access and manipulate these objects, change their properties, react to events, and even create new elements and attributes.

Understanding the DOM is fundamental to web development because it's the bridge between your HTML/CSS and JavaScript. Without the DOM, your JavaScript code wouldn't be able to interact with your web page's content and structure.

Section 1: Understanding the DOM

Welcome to the first section of our deep dive into the DOM! Now that we've covered the basics, let's roll up our sleeves and get into the nitty-gritty of what the DOM is, how it works, and its role in JavaScript.

What is the DOM?

The Document Object Model, or DOM, is a programming interface for HTML and XML documents. It represents the structure of a document in a way that's easy for programming languages like JavaScript to interact with. The DOM transforms your document into an object-oriented hierarchy or a "tree" of nodes. These nodes are objects that represent parts of the document, such as elements, attributes, and text.

Imagine a family tree. You have the grandparents at the top (the root node), their children below them (child nodes), and their grandchildren below them (leaf nodes). Similarly, in the DOM tree, the document object is the root node, elements like <body> and <head> are child nodes, and the elements and text inside them are leaf nodes.

How Does the DOM Work?

When you load a web page, the browser fetches the HTML and parses it, creating the DOM. This DOM is stored in the browser's memory, not as a string, but as a set of interconnected objects with properties and methods.

Let's take a look at a simple HTML document:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>My Web Page</title>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>Welcome to my web page!</h1>
    <p>This is a paragraph.</p>
</body>
</html>

On the other hand, the browser would create a DOM that looks something like this:

document
|-- html
    |-- head
        |-- title
            |-- "My Web Page"
    |-- body
        |-- h1
            |-- "Welcome to my web page!"
        |-- p
            |-- "This is a paragraph."

This tree-like structure is what JavaScript interacts with. It can access any node, change its contents, apply styles, add or remove nodes, and so on. For instance, if you want to change the text inside the <h1> tag or you can select the <body> node and add a new child node to it. You do many things and you use JavaScript to select these nodes in the DOM and manipulate its contents.

The Role of the DOM in JavaScript

The DOM is the meeting point between HTML and JavaScript. It's what allows JavaScript to interact with the HTML, changing the document's structure, content, and styling on the fly.

Without the DOM, your JavaScript code would have no way of accessing or manipulating the HTML. You wouldn't be able to respond to user events, update the content dynamically, or create interactive web experiences.

In other words, the DOM is what turns your static HTML document into a dynamic, interactive web page. It's the magic behind every JavaScript-powered website or web app you've ever used.

Section 2: Selecting Elements

Alright, now that we've got a solid understanding of what the DOM is, let's get our hands dirty and start interacting with it. The first step in DOM manipulation is selecting the elements you want to work with, and that's where querySelector and querySelectorAll come into play.

querySelector

querySelector is a powerful method that allows you to select the first element that matches a specified CSS selector(s) in the document. It's like a Swiss Army knife for DOM selection. You can use it to select elements by their tag name, class, ID, attribute, and more.

Here's how it works:

let element = document.querySelector(selector);

The selector is a string containing one or more CSS selectors separated by commas. querySelector returns the first element that matches any of the specified selectors. If no matches are found, it returns null.

Let's say we have the following HTML:

<div class="container">
    <h1 id="title">Hello, World!</h1>
    <p class="text">Welcome to my web page.</p>
</div>

Here's how you can use querySelector to select these above elements:

let title = document.querySelector('#title'); // selects the element with the ID "title"
let text = document.querySelector('.text'); // selects the element with the class "text"
let div = document.querySelector('div'); // selects the first <div> element

querySelectorAll

While querySelector returns the first matching element, querySelectorAll returns a NodeList (think of it as a collection) of all elements in the document that match the specified CSS selector(s).

Here's how you can use it:

let elements = document.querySelectorAll(selector);

Again, selector is a string containing one or more CSS selectors separated by commas. querySelectorAll returns a NodeList of all elements that match any of the specified selectors. If no matches are found, it returns an empty NodeList.

Using the same HTML as before, here's how you can use querySelectorAll:

let divs = document.querySelectorAll('div'); // selects all <div> elements
let elements = document.querySelectorAll('.text, #title'); // selects all elements with the class "text" and the ID "title"

Remember, querySelectorAll returns a NodeList, not an array. While you can use some array methods on a NodeList (like forEach), others (like map and filter) are not available. If you need to use these methods, you can convert the NodeList to an array using Array.from().

And there you have it! You now know how to select elements in the DOM using querySelector and querySelectorAll. These methods are incredibly powerful and versatile, and they're the foundation of all DOM manipulation. So go ahead, start selecting elements and playing around with them.

Section 3: DOM Most Important Properties & Methods

Welcome to the heart of DOM manipulation: properties and methods. Now that we know how to select elements, it's time to learn how to interact with them. In this section, we'll explore a variety of properties and methods that allow us to read, change, add, and remove elements and their content. Let's dive in!

classList

The classList property returns a live DOMTokenList collection of the class attributes of the element. This property is useful for adding, removing and toggling CSS classes on an element.

let element = document.querySelector('#myElement');
element.classList.add('new-class'); // adds 'new-class' to the element
element.classList.remove('new-class'); // removes 'new-class' from the element
element.classList.toggle('new-class'); // toggles 'new-class'

getAttribute() and setAttribute()

getAttribute() returns the value of a specified attribute on the element. setAttribute() sets the value of an attribute on the specified element.

let link = document.querySelector('a');
let href = link.getAttribute('href'); // gets the href attribute
link.setAttribute('href', 'https://www.example.com'); // sets the href attribute

appendChild(), append(), and prepend()

appendChild() adds a node to the end of the list of children of a specified parent node. append() and prepend() do the same but allow you to add multiple nodes, and they also accept strings to be inserted as text nodes.

let newElement = document.createElement('div');
document.body.appendChild(newElement); // appends newElement to the body

let textNode = document.createTextNode('Hello, World!');
newElement.append(textNode, ' Welcome!'); // appends two nodes to newElement

newElement.prepend('Greetings! '); // prepends a text node to newElement

removeChild() and remove()

removeChild() removes a child node from the DOM and returns the removed node. remove() removes the current node from the DOM.

let element = document.querySelector('#myElement');
document.body.removeChild(element); // removes element from the body

element.remove(); // removes element

createElement, innerText, textContent, and innerHTML

createElement() creates a new element node. innerText and textContent get or set the text content of a node and its descendants. innerHTML gets or sets the HTML content (inner HTML) of an element.

let newElement = document.createElement('div'); // creates a new <div> element

newElement.innerText = 'Hello, World!'; // sets the text content
console.log(newElement.textContent); // gets the text content

newElement.innerHTML = '<strong>Hello, World!</strong>'; // sets the HTML content

value, parentElement, children, nextSibling, previousSibling, and style

value gets or sets the value of the value attribute of a text field. parentElement returns the parent element of the specified element. children returns a live HTMLCollection of child elements. nextSibling and previousSibling return the next and previous sibling of the node in the tree, or null if there are no siblings. style gets or sets the inline style of an element.

let input = document.querySelector('input');
input.value = 'Hello, World!'; // sets the value

let parent = element.parentElement; // gets the parent element
let children = element.children; // gets the child elements

let next = element.nextSibling; // gets the next sibling
let previous = element.previousSibling

Section 4: Event Handling and Most Common Events in JavaScript

Welcome to the exciting world of event handling in JavaScript! This is where your web pages truly come to life, responding to user interactions in real time. From simple clicks to complex drags and drops, JavaScript events are the heart of interactive web experiences. Let's dive in!

What is Event Handling?

In JavaScript, an event is a signal that something has happened. This could be a user action, like clicking a button or submitting a form, or a browser action, like loading a page or resizing a window.

Event handling is the process of setting up listeners on elements that respond to these events. When an event occurs, the listener executes a JavaScript function, known as an event handler.

Event handling is crucial in JavaScript because it allows your web pages to respond to user interactions and create dynamic, interactive experiences.

Common JavaScript Events

Let's take a look at some common JavaScript events and how to handle them.

Clicks

The click event fires when a user clicks an element.

let button = document.querySelector('button');
button.addEventListener('click', function() {
    console.log('Button clicked!');
});

Drags and Drops

The dragstart, dragover, and drop events are used to create drag and drop functionality.

let draggable = document.querySelector('.draggable');
let dropzone = document.querySelector('.dropzone');

draggable.addEventListener('dragstart', function(event) {
    event.dataTransfer.setData('text/plain', draggable.id);
});

dropzone.addEventListener('dragover', function(event) {
    event.preventDefault(); // prevent default to allow drop
});

dropzone.addEventListener('drop', function(event) {
    event.preventDefault(); // prevent default action (open as link for some elements)
    let id = event.dataTransfer.getData('text');
    let draggable = document.getElementById(id);
    dropzone.appendChild(draggable);
});

Hovers

The mouseover and mouseout events fire when the mouse pointer enters and leaves an element, respectively.

let element = document.querySelector('#myElement');
element.addEventListener('mouseover', function() {
    element.style.backgroundColor = 'yellow';
});
element.addEventListener('mouseout', function() {
    element.style.backgroundColor = '';
});

The scroll event fires when an element's scrollbar is being scrolled.

window.addEventListener('scroll', function() {
    console.log('Scrolling!');
});

The submit event fires when a form is submitted.

let form = document.querySelector('form');
form.addEventListener('submit', function(event) {
    event.preventDefault(); // prevent form from submitting normally
    console.log('Form submitted!');
});

Key Presses

The keydown, keypress, and keyup events fire when a user is pressing or releasing a key.

window.addEventListener('keydown', function(event) {
    console.log('Key pressed: ' + event.key);
});

Focus/Blur

The focus and blur events fire when an element gets or loses focus.

let input = document.querySelector('input');
input.addEventListener('focus', function() {
    console.log('Input focused!');
});
input.addEventListener('blur', function() {
    console.log('Input blurred!');
});

Double Click

The dblclick event fires when an element is double-clicked.

let button = document.querySelector('button');
button.addEventListener('dblclick',

Section 5: Asynchronous JavaScript and the DOM

As we near the end of our deep dive into the DOM, let's take a moment to peek into the future. Specifically, let's talk about asynchronous JavaScript and how it interacts with the DOM. This is a vast topic that deserves its own series of articles, but we'll touch on it briefly here to give you a taste of what's to come.

In JavaScript, most operations are synchronous, meaning they block further execution until they're finished. However, certain operations, like fetching data from a server or setting a timer, are asynchronous. They're initiated now, but they finish later, allowing the rest of your code to continue running in the meantime.

Asynchronous JavaScript is crucial for creating smooth, responsive web experiences. It allows your web page to stay interactive even when it's performing time-consuming tasks, like loading data or images.

Here's a simple example of asynchronous JavaScript in action:

console.log('Fetching data...');

fetch('https://api.example.com/data')
    .then(response => response.json())
    .then(data => {
        console.log('Data fetched!');
        // Now you can use the data to update the DOM
    });

console.log('This will run before the data is fetched!');

In this example, the fetch function returns a Promise, which is a placeholder for the future result of an operation. The then method is used to schedule callbacks to be run when the Promise is fulfilled, or in other words, when the data is fetched.

This is just the tip of the iceberg when it comes to asynchronous JavaScript. There's so much more to learn, including callbacks, Promises, async/await, and more. But don't worry, we'll cover all of that and more in future articles. For now, just know that asynchronous JavaScript is a powerful tool for creating dynamic, interactive web experiences, and it plays a crucial role in DOM manipulation.

Stay tuned for more, and keep practicing what you've learned so far. You're well on your way to becoming a DOM manipulation master!

Section 6: Common Pitfalls in DOM Manipulation

Alright, we've covered a lot of ground so far. We've learned what the DOM is, how to select elements, and how to use properties and methods to manipulate those elements. Now, it's time to talk about some common pitfalls in DOM manipulation. Even experienced developers can fall into these traps, so let's shine a light on them and learn how to avoid them.

Pitfall 1: Not Waiting for the DOM to Load

One of the most common mistakes is trying to manipulate the DOM before it's fully loaded. If your JavaScript runs before the HTML is parsed, it won't be able to find the elements it's supposed to interact with.

To avoid this, you can use the DOMContentLoaded event, which fires when the initial HTML document has been completely loaded and parsed, without waiting for stylesheets, images, and subframes to finish loading.

document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() {
    // Your code here
});

Pitfall 2: Overusing innerHTML

While innerHTML is a powerful property that allows you to get or set the HTML content of an element, it can be a source of performance issues and security vulnerabilities.

Every time you use innerHTML, the browser has to parse the new HTML string into a DOM tree, which can be slow for large amounts of HTML. It also exposes you to cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks if you're not careful with user input.
